html:
"No one can change the words of Allah" — yet tahrif is the central Muslim claim about the Bible Logical Inconsistency Jesus / Christology Strong Quran 6:115
"And the word of your Lord has been fulfilled in truth and in justice. None can alter His words..." (6:115)
"...no change is there in the words of Allah." (10:64)

What the verses say

The Quran repeatedly and emphatically states that no one can alter the words of Allah — presented as proof of divine reliability. But the standard Muslim explanation for why the Bible contradicts the Quran is tahrif: Jews and Christians corrupted their scriptures. The Torah and Gospel were, per the Quran itself (Q 5:43–48, 3:3), words revealed by Allah.

Why this is a problem

Islam cannot consistently hold both: (a) "Allah's words are unchangeable" and (b) "the Bible is corrupted." If no one can change Allah's words, the Bible cannot have been corrupted. If it was corrupted, then humans did change Allah's words — falsifying the Quran's own preservation claim. Each rescue attempt weakens the original claim further.

Why it fails

Q 6:115 and Q 10:64 make unqualified claims — "none can alter His words" with no conditional about unfaithful communities. The meaning-only reading of tahrif means the Bible's physical words are Allah's unchanged words, including the crucifixion, Trinity, and sonship of Jesus — which directly contradicts the Quran. If Allah failed to preserve prior scriptures despite promising preservation, the same failure could apply to the Quran.

Skins roasted and replaced — eternal torture engineered for maximum pain Logical Inconsistency Treatment of Disbelievers Strong Quran 4:56
"Indeed, those who disbelieve in Our verses — We will drive them into a fire. Every time their skins are roasted through We will replace them with other skins so they may taste the punishment. Indeed, Allah is ever Exalted in Might and Wise."

What the verse says

Allah burns disbelievers eternally. When their skin is destroyed and nerve endings can no longer feel pain, He replaces the skin with fresh skin — so pain resumes at full intensity. This cycle never ends.

Why this is a problem

This is a mechanical description of how Allah engineers maximum suffering. The verse specifically highlights skin-replacement as the solution to a pain-tolerance problem — a design feature to defeat the natural mercy of nerve damage. A finite creature cannot commit infinite wrong; a 70-year life of unbelief cannot morally warrant unending torture. The verse shows Allah anticipating that normal burning would eventually numb sufferers and correcting for that — not impersonal justice but sadistic redesign.

Why it fails

The "choice" involved is rejecting a specific Arabic revelation delivered in the 7th century — one billions of humans either never heard or had prior rational grounds to regard as unconvincing. Punishing them eternally for this is not justice; it is rigged justice. The hadith corpus spends enormous detail on the physical torments of Hell, and the mainstream Sunni position has never been metaphorical. Softening the verse requires abandoning fourteen centuries of traditional reading.

Good from Allah, evil from yourself — direct contradiction two verses apart Contradiction Logical Inconsistency Strong Quran 4:78 vs 4:79
"...Say, 'All [things] are from Allah.'" (4:78)
"What comes to you of good is from Allah, but what comes to you of evil, [O man], is from yourself..." (4:79)

What the verses say

Verse 78: whatever happens — good or evil — is from Allah. Verse 79, immediately following: good is from Allah; evil is from yourself. Both verses use the same vocabulary, address the same question (where does evil come from?), and give opposite answers — just lines above Q 4:82's self-test ("no contradictions if from Allah").

Why this is a problem

This is one of the clearest textual contradictions in the Quran. The two verses are adjacent, use the general word sayyi'ah (bad thing/misfortune) in the same context, and produce flatly incompatible causal claims about the origin of evil. Q 4:82's self-test is one of the casualties.

Why it fails

The creation/acquisition distinction does not appear in either verse — it was invented by theologians centuries later to manage this problem. Both use the same word sayyi'ah in the same context about events happening to people. The verses contradict each other, and the self-test in Q 4:82 is directly implicated by their adjacency.

"You will never be able to be just between wives" — yet polygamy stays authorised Contradiction Women Strong Quran 4:3 vs 4:129
"...marry those that please you of [other] women, two or three or four. But if you fear that you will not be just, then [marry only] one..." (4:3)
"And you will never be able to be equal [in feeling] between wives, even if you should strive [to do so]..." (4:129)

What the verses say

Q 4:3 permits polygamy up to four wives — conditional on the husband being just among them. Q 4:129 declares flatly that a man will never be able to be just between wives, no matter how hard he tries.

Why this is a problem

The condition for permission (Q 4:3) is declared impossible in Q 4:129. If justice between wives is the prerequisite, and justice cannot be achieved, then polygamy cannot validly be practised. Yet it remains lawful across the Islamic world. Either reading is uncomfortable: take Q 4:129 at face value and polygamy is functionally forbidden; take Q 4:3 at face value and Q 4:129 becomes mere hyperbole. The Quran's own "self-test" verse (Q 4:82) is implicated within the same surah.

Why it fails

The distinction is interpretively possible but textually invented — neither verse draws it. A book that claims to be "clear" (Q 11:1) should not require imported theological scaffolding to avoid contradicting itself within the same surah. The more honest reading is that Q 4:129 concedes what Q 4:3 demanded: perfect justice between wives is not humanly achievable.

Amputation, crucifixion, or exile — penalty for "waging war against Allah" Treatment of Disbelievers Women Strong Quran 5:33–34
"Indeed, the penalty for those who wage war against Allah and His Messenger and strive upon earth [to cause] corruption is none but that they be killed or crucified or that their hands and feet be cut off from opposite sides or that they be exiled from the land..."

What the verse says

For "waging war against Allah and His Messenger" and "corruption on earth," the Quran prescribes a menu: execution, crucifixion, alternating-sides amputation, or exile. ISIS cited Q 5:33 as legal basis for public crucifixions and hand-foot amputations in Syria and Iraq between 2014 and 2019.

Why this is a problem

  1. The crimes are undefined — "waging war against Allah" and "corruption on earth" have been stretched by classical jurists to cover highway robbery, apostasy, heresy, armed rebellion, and in modern states, drug trafficking and dissent.
  2. Crucifixion and alternating-sides amputation are theatrical punishments designed for maximal visible horror — public-display punishment to terrify populations, not proportional justice.

Why it fails

The verse explicitly addresses a general category — "those who wage war against Allah and His Messenger" — not just the Urayna. Classical Muslim legal scholarship treated it as general legislation for all time, which is why it appears in Sharia codes across multiple countries today. "Originally specific" does not change how the tradition has read, codified, and applied it for 1,400 years.

The Night Journey — Muhammad flies to Jerusalem on a winged creature Strange / Obscure Prophetic Character Strong Quran 17:1 (with hadith Bukhari 3887)
"Exalted is He who took His Servant by night from al-Masjid al-Haram to al-Masjid al-Aqsa, whose surroundings We have blessed, to show him of Our signs..."

What the verse says

In a single night, Allah transports Muhammad from Mecca to "al-Masjid al-Aqsa." The hadith tradition elaborates that Muhammad rode the winged creature Buraq, met earlier prophets, ascended through seven heavens, and negotiated the number of daily prayers from 50 down to 5 with Moses.

Why this is a problem

  1. There was no Al-Aqsa Mosque in 621 CE — the current mosque was built in 705 CE, over 80 years after the Night Journey. The Temple Mount held ruins of the Jewish Temple, not a mosque.
  2. Muhammad's own contemporaries reacted with disbelief; many apostatised (Bukhari 3886). Abu Bakr earned the title "al-Siddiq" (the faithful) specifically for accepting the story — the honorific signals the claim demanded extraordinary trust.

Why it fails

Mainstream Islamic tradition pins the destination to a specific physical location in Jerusalem, forming the basis of Islam's territorial claim on the site. The "spiritual vision" reading was held by some early scholars but rejected by the majority, and retreating to it now to avoid the historical problem is modern apologetics against the classical doctrine. The prayer-negotiation story (Moses tells Muhammad to keep bargaining down from 50 prayers) is particularly difficult to read as spiritually edifying.

"Strike their necks" — the beheading command Treatment of Disbelievers Strong Quran 47:4
"So when you meet those who disbelieve [in battle], strike [their] necks until, when you have inflicted slaughter upon them, then secure their bonds, and either [confer] favor afterwards or ransom [them] until the war lays down its burdens..."

What the verse says

When Muslims meet disbelievers in battle: behead until you have inflicted sufficient slaughter, then and only then take captives. Survivors may be freed or ransomed. The priority is killing first, captive-taking second — offensive choreography, not defensive framing.

Why this is a problem

The verse does not say "defeat them in battle" — it specifies the technique ("strike the necks") and sets a killing threshold before captive-taking begins. Modern jihadist movements cite Q 47:4 directly and do not need to distort the text. The verse historically provided scriptural warrant for ritualized beheading from the early caliphates through Saudi judicial beheadings and ISIS execution videos.

Why it fails

The idiom itself is violent and specific, and the classical tradition has read and applied it literally in military contexts. The "battlefield only" defense is technically correct — Q 47:4 concerns combat — but it establishes that beheading is the prescribed method and that killing precedes capture. Limiting it to Muhammad's specific wars is a modern apologetic move against the classical reading that treated it as permanent Islamic war law.

"Do not take the Jews and Christians as allies" Treatment of Disbelievers Strong Quran 5:51
"O you who have believed, do not take the Jews and the Christians as allies... And whoever is an ally to them among you — then indeed, he is [one] of them."

What the verse says

Muslims are forbidden from taking Jews and Christians as allies (awliya'). Taking them as allies makes one of them. The Arabic awliya' covers a broad range: alliance, friendship, protection, trust, patronage. Classical tafsir (Tabari, Ibn Kathir) read the prohibition broadly, and modern conservative Muslim discourse continues to apply it to personal interfaith friendship.

Why this is a problem

A religion that forbids friendship across religious lines at the scriptural level has baked inter-communal hostility into its foundation. The verse names two specific religious communities as off-limits — not hostile individuals, but religious categories — embedding identity politics into its ethical framework at the textual level.

Why it fails

The narrow-military reading is the modern apologetic move that requires reading against the classical grain. Classical tafsir applied the prohibition broadly to personal as well as political relationships, and modern conservative discourse follows that reading. A religion whose founding scripture prohibits religious-category alliance has embedded this hostility at the textual level regardless of how reformists reframe it.

Allah asks Jesus: "Did you tell people to take you and your mother as gods?" Jesus / Christology Contradiction Strong Quran 5:116
"O Jesus, Son of Mary, did you say to the people, 'Take me and my mother as deities besides Allah?'"

What the verse says

Allah confronts Jesus about whether he instructed people to worship him and Mary as two gods alongside Allah. Jesus denies this. The Christian Trinity is Father, Son, and Holy Spirit — not Father, Mary, and Jesus. No Christian sect has ever defined the Trinity as including Mary.

Why this is a problem

The verse misrepresents the doctrine it attempts to refute. A divine text correcting Christian theology should engage the Christianity Christians actually confess, not a different three-deity schema no Christian has held. The Quran substitutes Mary for the Holy Spirit and presents the substitution as the target doctrine — which is a factual error about Christian belief.

Why it fails

The Collyridian hypothesis rests on a sect attested only in Epiphanius's Panarion and never evidenced as widespread in 7th-century Arabia. Orthodox Christianity — Catholic, Protestant, Eastern Orthodox — has never defined the Trinity as Father/Mary/Jesus. If the Quran were addressing functional rather than official theology, the text should say so. An omniscient God correcting Christian doctrine is accountable to the doctrine Christians actually hold.

Jesus prophesied "Ahmad" — no Gospel records this Jesus / Christology Strong Quran 61:6
"Jesus said: 'I am the messenger of Allah to you... bringing good tidings of a messenger to come after me, whose name is Ahmad.'"

What the verse says

Jesus foretells a prophet named "Ahmad" (identified with Muhammad) who will come after him. No Gospel contains this prophecy. No early Christian document records Jesus promising a future prophet named Ahmad or any Arabic equivalent.

Why this is a problem

The only evidence for this prophecy is Islam's own text — a circularly-attested claim with no independent corroboration. Classical apologetics links "Ahmad" to John 14:16's "Paraclete" (Parakletos), arguing that Parakletos ("comforter") is a mistranscription of Periklytos ("praised," analogous to Arabic Ahmad) — implying early Christian manuscript corruption obscured the original prophecy.

Why it fails

No Greek manuscript evidence supports Periklytos anywhere in early copies of John 14. Every surviving early Greek manuscript reads Parakletos. The early church fathers consistently identified the Paraclete as the Holy Spirit, already sent at Pentecost (Acts 2). A prophecy requiring a conjectured mis-spelling unattested in any manuscript is not prediction — it is retroactive construction.

"Stay in your houses and do not display yourselves" Women Strong Quran 33:33
"Abide in your houses and do not display yourselves as [was] the display of the former times of ignorance."

What the verse says

Originally addressed to the Prophet's wives; extended by classical tradition to all Muslim women as the textual warrant for public-space restrictions. Modern Saudi-style confinement, Taliban-era Afghan home-confinement policies, and Iranian public-attire laws all cite this verse. The verse's framing — "former times of ignorance" — presupposes that pre-Islamic female public life was degraded, advancing a specific social model that has constrained women across fourteen centuries.

Why this is a problem

Classical jurisprudence across all four Sunni schools consistently extended the principle beyond Muhammad's wives to all Muslim women, treating Q 33:33 as the textual foundation for female public-life restrictions. The "former times of ignorance" framing is itself the problem — it frames female public presence as a pre-Islamic defect to be corrected, embedding gender restriction as the Islamic ideal.

Why it fails

The "only Muhammad's wives" narrowing is modern reformist work against the classical grain. The same Sunni legal schools that produced Islamic jurisprudence universally extended the verse's principle, and states implementing Sharia today cite it as standing divine instruction. The "former times of ignorance" framing remains in the text regardless of how narrowly one reads the original address.

Allah threatens Muhammad's wives with replacement — including virgins Women Strong Quran 66:5
"Perhaps his Lord, if he divorced you, would substitute for him wives better than you — submitting, believing... previously married and virgins."

What the verse says

After the Mariyah/honey dispute, Allah warns Muhammad's wives: comply or Allah will provide better replacements, specifying both previously married women and virgins. The trigger is Muhammad's wives objecting to his intimate relations with a slave concubine in their domestic space.

Why this is a problem

Divine authority is deployed to threaten women who objected to their husband sleeping with a slave. Aisha's documented observation — "I see your Lord hastens to fulfill your desires" — captures the structural problem: revelations concerning the Prophet's domestic arrangements consistently resolve in his favour. The verse gives no weight to the wives' perspective and treats divine threat of replacement as the appropriate response to women voicing domestic grievances.

Why it fails

The "special responsibilities" framing does not remove what the verse does: it deploys divine authority to threaten women who objected to their husband sleeping with a slave. The pattern across Q 33:37 (Zaynab), Q 33:50 (special marriage permissions), and Q 66:1–5 (Mariyah) is consistent — each time a personal situation favours Muhammad, a revelation arrives to validate it. Aisha's inside-the-household observation is the most honest commentary the tradition has produced on these verses.
